title: One Seat Ride Act
plain_summary: This bill tells the Transportation Secretary to study commuter trains that let people ride all the way to their destination without switching trains. The study must look at the good things and problems with this kind of train service. It needs to pay special attention to the New Jersey Transit Raritan Valley train line. The study must be finished and sent to Congress within one year.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Railroads, Pipelines, and Hazardous Materials.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the One Seat Ride Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Study on Commuter Service
sections.text:
The Secretary of Transportation must do a study about commuter rail trains. Commuter rail trains are trains that carry passengers to and from work in cities and nearby areas. The study must find out what good things happen when people can ride these trains. It must also find out what problems make it hard to let passengers ride from start to finish without having to change trains.

When doing this study, the Secretary must think about money costs, how hard it would be to organize, and how it would affect people's daily lives. The Secretary must look at what stops people from being able to ride straight through without changing trains. The study must also look closely at one specific train line called the New Jersey Transit Raritan Valley line. For this line, the Secretary must study what it would cost and what benefits there would be if passengers could ride straight through during the busiest travel times. The Secretary must also study how this would affect other New Jersey Transit train lines.

The Secretary must write a report about this study. This report must be given to two groups in Congress within one year after this law is passed. These groups are the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure in the House of Representatives and the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs in the Senate.

In this section, commuter rail passenger transportation means what that term means in section twenty four thousand one hundred two of title forty nine of the United States Code.
